findings.description: login() builds the SQL query via f-string interpolation of the user-supplied email, allowing SQL injection and authentication bypass (e.g. email = ' OR '1'='1' --).

validate_session inverts the expiry check: when expires_at is in the future (live session) it deletes it and returns None, and for expired sessions it returns the user_id, so live sessions are rejected and expired sessions are accepted.
find_user_by_email runs SELECT * FROM users and loads every row into memory before a Python-side linear scan, instead of a WHERE email = ? lookup — O(n) memory/transfer and a DoS/scaling risk.

LOCKOUT_MINUTES is defined but never used; failed_attempts is never time-decayed, so a locked account (failed_attempts >= MAX_FAILED_ATTEMPTS) stays locked permanently rather than for 15 minutes as the constant implies.
datetime.utcnow() is deprecated (3.12+) and returns naive datetimes; comparisons in validate_session/cleanup_expired break if any timezone-aware datetime is ever introduced.
Sessions are stored only in the process-local self._sessions dict, so they are lost on restart and not shared across workers/processes, contradicting a durable 'session management service' contract.

Unknown-email path returns before any password hashing while a known email runs PBKDF2, and 'account locked' vs 'unknown email' produce distinct observable outcomes, enabling user enumeration via timing/behavior.
The failed_attempts SELECT/UPDATE/reset sequence is not atomic, so concurrent login attempts can race and lose updates to the counter.
PBKDF2-HMAC-SHA256 at 100,000 iterations is below current OWASP guidance (~600,000), weakening resistance to offline brute-force.
The 'session expired' log fires on the branch that actually deletes a live (non-expired) session, producing misleading audit logs even relative to the intended behavior.
